subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{4}{K^2+5 k+6} \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{4}{(K+2)(K+3)}=4 \sum_{K=1}^n\left(\frac{1}{K+2}-\frac{1}{K+3}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =4\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{5}\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{1}{n+2}-\frac{1}{n+3}\right] \\ & S_n=4\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{n+3}\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & S_{2025}=4\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2028}\right] \\ & S_{2025}=4\left[\frac{675}{2028}\right] \\ & 507 S_{2025}=675\end{aligned}$

solution:
$\Delta=\frac{1}{2} p_1 a=\frac{1}{2} p_2 b=\frac{1}{2} p_3 b$
$p_1, p_2, p_3$ are in H.P.
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \Delta}{a}, \frac{2 \Delta}{b}, \frac{2 \Delta}{c}$ are in H.P.
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}$ are in H.P
$\Rightarrow a, b, c$ are in A.P.
$\Rightarrow \sin A, \sin B, \sin C$ are in A.P.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The sum has $n$ terms: $\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x+2k-2)(x+2k)$.
Expanding and summing gives $nx^2 + 2n^2x + \frac{4n(n^2-1)}{3} = \frac{8n}{3}$.
Dividing by $n$ and simplifying: $3x^2 + 6nx + 4n^2 - 12 = 0$.
Discriminant $= 36n^2 - 12(4n^2 - 12) = 12(12 - n^2)$.
Difference of roots $= \frac{2\sqrt{3(12-n^2)}}{3}$.
For two consecutive even integers, this difference $= 2$.
$\sqrt{36 - 3n^2} = 3 \Rightarrow n^2 = 9 \Rightarrow n = 3$.
For $n = 3$: $x = \frac{-9 \pm 3}{3} = -2$ or $-4$, which are consecutive even integers.

Therefore, the required answer is 223.

solution:
Let the three numbers of the GP be a, ar and ar2 where r is the common ratio.
According to the given condition, a, 2ar and ar2 are in AP.

∴  2ar=a+ar22

∴  a+ar2=4ar

∴  r2+1=4r

∴  r2-4r+1=0

∴  r=4±16-42

         = 4 ± 2 3 2 = 2 ± 3

As it is an 'increasing' GP, r > 1.

∴  r=2+3.

d=93=3
Sum of last 4 terms is178.
4l-6d=178
l=49

10+6⋅3+10+7⋅32
=592 =29.5

Alternate way

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $b_n = a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}$.
$b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}a_n + \frac{n^2-2n-1}{n^2(n+1)^2} - \frac{2}{(n+1)^2}$.
Simplifying: $b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}b_n + \frac{(n+1)^2 + n^2 - 2n - 1 - 2n^2}{n^2(n+1)^2} = \frac{1}{2}b_n$.
So $b_n$ is GP with ratio $\frac{1}{2}$.
$b_1 = 1 - 2 = -1$, so $b_n = -\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1}$.
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n = -\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{2}} = -2$.
$\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}\right)\right| = 2$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $a, a r, a r^2, \ldots$
$a+a r=12$
$a r^2+a r^3=48$
dividing (2) by (1), we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{a r^2(1+r)}{a(r+1)}=4 \\
& \Rightarrow r^2=4 \text { if } r \neq-1 \\
& \therefore r=-2 \\
& \text { also, } a=-12 \quad \text { (using (1)). }
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Consider $1^2+3^2+5^2+\ldots \ldots+25^2$ $n^{\text {th }}$ term $\mathrm{T}_n=(2 n-1)^2, n=1, \ldots \ldots 13$
Now, $\mathrm{S}_n=\sum_{n=1}^{13} \mathrm{~T}_n=\sum_{n=1}^{13}(2 n-1)^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\sum_{n=1}^{13} 4 n^2+\sum_{n=1}^{13} 1-\sum_{n=1}^{13} 4 n \\
&=4 \sum n^2+13-4 \sum n
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
=4\left[\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}\right]+13-4 \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$
Put $n=13$, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{S}_n=26 \times 14 \times 9+13-26 \times 14 \\
&=3276+13-364 \\
&=2925 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution: Area of first $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4}$ Area of second $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4} \frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{16}$ Area of third $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{64}$ sum of area $=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4}\left(1+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16} \ldots\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4} \frac{1}{\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 1^{\text {st }} \Delta=3 \mathrm{a} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 2^{\text {nd }} \Delta=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 3^{\text {rd }} \Delta=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{a}\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\ldots\right) \\ & \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{a} \cdot 2=6 \mathrm{a} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{6} \\ & \mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{36} \\ & \mathrm{P}^2=36 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{Q}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
In the G.P., let the first three terms be $\frac{a}{r}, a, ar$.
From the product condition: $\frac{a}{r} \cdot a \cdot ar = a^3 = 27$, so $a = 3$.
The sum of first three terms is $S = \frac{3}{r} + 3 + 3r = 3(\frac{1}{r} + 1 + r)$.
For $r > 0$, by AM-GM: $\frac{1}{r} + r \geq 2$, so $S \geq 9$.
For $r < 0$: $\frac{1}{r} + r \leq -2$.
So $S \leq -3$.
Thus the possible values of $S$ are $(-\infty, -3] \cup [9, \infty) = \mathbb{R} - (-3, 9)$.
Therefore $(a, b) = (-3, 9)$ and $a^2 + b^2 = 9 + 81 = 90$.

Also, 2y2=xz .......ii

Also given that xy+yz+zx=32xyz
⇒1x+1y+1z=32 .....iii
From (i) and (iii) we get 3y=32

y=2 .....iv

Now from (ii) xz=4  .......v

Now using (ii), (iv) and (v)

⇒x+z=42

Hence 3(x+y+z)2=3(2+42)2

=150

Therefore, this is the required answer.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given sum is
$S_n=1+3+11+25+45+71+\ldots+T_n$
First order differences are in A.P.
Thus, we can assume that
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{an}^2+\mathrm{bn}+\mathrm{c}$
Solving $\left\{\begin{array}{c}T_1=1=a+b+c \\ T_2=3=4 a+2 b+c \\ T_3=11=9 a+3 b+c\end{array}\right\}$,
we get $\mathrm{a}=3, \mathrm{~b}=-7, \mathrm{c}=5$
Hence, general term of given series is
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=3 \mathrm{n}^2-7 \mathrm{n}+5$
Hence, required sum equals
$\sum_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{\mathrm{n}=20}\left(3 \mathrm{n}^2-7 \mathrm{n}+5\right)=3\left(\frac{20 \cdot 21 \cdot 41}{6}\right)-7\left(\frac{20 \cdot 21}{2}\right)+5(20)=7240$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The set $A$ consists of the first 101 terms of an A.P. with first term $a_1 = 1$ and common difference $d_1 = 5$.

The terms of $A$ are $1, 6, 11, 16, \dots, 1 + (101-1) \times 5 = 501$.

The set $B$ consists of the first 71 terms of an A.P. with first term $b_1 = 9$ and common difference $d_2 = 7$.

The terms of $B$ are $9, 16, 23, 30, \dots, 9 + (71-1) \times 7 = 499$.

The first common term of $A$ and $B$ is $16$.

The common difference of the terms in $A \cap B$ is the least common multiple of $d_1$ and $d_2$, which is $\text{lcm}(5, 7) = 35$.

The general term of $A \cap B$ is given by $T_k = 16 + 35k$, where $k \ge 0$ is an integer.

Since the maximum possible value in $A \cap B$ cannot exceed the maximum value in $B$ (which is $499$), we have:

$16 + 35k \le 499$

$35k \le 483$

$k \le 13.8$

Thus, the possible values for $k$ are $0, 1, 2, \dots, 13$.

We require the terms in $A \cap B$ to be divisible by $3$. Therefore:

$16 + 35k \equiv 0 \pmod 3$

$1 + 2k \equiv 0 \pmod 3$

$2k \equiv 2 \pmod 3$

$k \equiv 1 \pmod 3$

The values of $k$ in the range $0 \le k \le 13$ that satisfy $k \equiv 1 \pmod 3$ are $1, 4, 7, 10, 13$.

There are exactly $5$ such values of $k$, which correspond to $5$ elements in $A \cap B$ that are divisible by $3$.

Answer: $5$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given series is $S = 1^3 - 2^3 + 3^3 - 4^3 + \ldots + 15^3$.

This can be rewritten as the sum of all cubes up to $15$ minus twice the sum of cubes of even numbers up to $14$:

$S = (1^3 + 2^3 + 3^3 + \ldots + 15^3) - 2(2^3 + 4^3 + 6^3 + \ldots + 14^3)$

$S = \sum_{k=1}^{15} k^3 - 2 \times 2^3 (1^3 + 2^3 + 3^3 + \ldots + 7^3)$

$S = \sum_{k=1}^{15} k^3 - 16 \sum_{k=1}^{7} k^3$

Using the formula for the sum of cubes $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k^3 = \left( \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \right)^2$:

$\sum_{k=1}^{15} k^3 = \left( \dfrac{15 \times 16}{2} \right)^2 = (120)^2 = 14400$

$\sum_{k=1}^{7} k^3 = \left( \dfrac{7 \times 8}{2} \right)^2 = (28)^2 = 784$

Substituting these values back into the expression for $S$:

$S = 14400 - 16 \times 784$

$S = 14400 - 12544 = 1856$

Answer: $1856$
